I'm making my famous three-layer lasagna tonight, but I'm not sure why the ricotta cheese has to be drained before mixing it with parmesan and egg. Is that like, to get rid of the extra water or something?

Draining the ricotta is about removing excess liquid so it mixes smoothly with other ingredients. If you leave too much water in the ricotta, it'll be hard to spread evenly between layers, and the cheese might separate when cooked. That's what makes your lasagna look blotchy sometimes.
"Wait, so you're saying I should squeeze out some of the liquid from the ricotta like I do with wet towels? Is that why my lasagnas have been looking a little messy lately?"

You don't need to squeeze it like a towel, just gently spread the ricotta on paper towels or a clean kitchen towel. The excess liquid will absorb and you'll be left with a smooth mixture. This way, your layers will look even and your lasagna won't fall apart when cut.
So I don't have to press down on the ricotta like it owes me money? That makes sense, 'cause if I squeeze too hard, it'll just turn into mush.

No, you don't want to squish the ricotta too much, or it will lose its texture and break apart in the layers. Just let it sit on the paper towels for a minute or two to absorb the excess liquid. This way, your lasagna will have nice, even layers when it's done.

"Hey, I'm making chicken parmesan tonight and I want to get the flavors right... what's with all this talk about 'balance' in recipes? Is it like how we balance our check at the grocery store?"

Flavor balance isn't like balancing a check. It means combining ingredients so they work together, not overpower each other. Think of it like seasoning: salt lifts flavors, acid cuts richness, and fat carries those flavors – we want them in harmony, not fighting with each other.

So it's like creating a team where everyone plays their part nicely? Like, the salt is the coach, acid is the referee, and fat is the quarterback... but instead of winning games, they make food taste good together?

That's a fun way to think about it! Think of the team dynamic, but instead of winning games, they harmonize flavors. Salt lifts, acid cuts richness, and fat carries those flavors – that's flavor balance in action.
So like, my flavors are a basketball team? And salt is the point guard who makes everyone else move smoothly?

You're getting close to understanding flavor balance. Instead of thinking about individual positions, consider how each ingredient works with others to create harmony. For your chicken parmesan, think about how the acidity in the tomato sauce helps cut through the richness of the cheese and meat, while the breadcrumbs add crunch and texture – that's a well-balanced team working together.
